facts.detail: Street dance starts January 22. City fiesta peaks on January 25, feast of the Conversion of St. Paul the Apostle. Activities often run from January 17 onwards.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Ilocos Sur, northern Luzon. Main events happen along Plaza Burgos, Plaza Salcedo, and the cobblestone Calle Crisologo.

facts.label: Patron
facts.detail: The fiesta is tied to the feast of the Conversion of St. Paul. The Metropolitan Cathedral is the seat of the Archdiocese of Nueva Segovia.

facts.label: Star Dish
facts.detail: Garlicky, slightly tangy pork sausage made with sukang Iloko vinegar. Registered as Vigan City's One Town, One Product (OTOP).

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Held annually in late April since 2016. From 1979 until 2015 it ran every three years.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Highland town in the Cordillera mountains of northern Luzon, around 350 kilometers north of Manila.

facts.label: Rooted in
facts.detail: Based on the old Ifugao ceremony marking a family's rise to the kadangyan (noble) class through abundant rice wine.

facts.label: Signature event
facts.detail: A 7-kilometer downhill race on handmade taltallak scooters carved from mananor and butbuta wood.

history.heading: From a kadangyan ritual to a town-wide festival
history.body:
Imbayah comes from bayah, the Ifugao word for rice wine. In old practice, a family that wanted to be recognized as kadangyan, the local noble class, had to host a long ceremony where rice wine was poured so generously that the jar was said to overflow. That image of abundance gave the rite its name and its meaning.

The public festival version was first organized in December 1979 under then Banaue Mayor Adriano Apilis Jr. The goal was practical. Tourism around the rice terraces was growing fast and elders were worried that young Ifugaos were losing the old chants, weaving patterns, and woodcarving skills. For decades after, Imbayah was held every three years.

In 2016, under Mayor Jerry U. Dalipog, the local government turned it into a yearly event so the cultural practices would be performed more often and so visitors had a reliable date to plan around. Sources: Rappler feature on Imbayah, Inquirer culture desk, and SunStar Baguio coverage of the shift to a yearly schedule.

key_events.name: Cultural Parade of the Barangays
key_events.blurb: The 12 barangays of Banaue march in five clustered groups, each in full native attire with gongs and chants.

key_events.name: Wooden Scooter Race
key_events.blurb: Around 7 kilometers of downhill mountain road on handmade taltallak scooters carved from mananor and butbuta wood.

key_events.name: Munpaot Woodcarving Competition
key_events.blurb: Carvers work live in front of the crowd, shaping bululs and other traditional pieces from a single block.

key_events.name: Rice Pounding and Bayah Sharing
key_events.blurb: Communal rice pounding followed by tasting of bayah, the fermented rice wine that gives the festival its name.

key_events.name: Boar Sacrifice Ritual
key_events.blurb: A public ritual rooted in the original kadangyan ceremony, performed by elders before the main program.

key_events.name: Mr. and Ms. Imbayah Pageant
key_events.blurb: Younger Ifugaos compete in traditional dress, with rounds on weaving, dance, and oral history.

place_intro:
Imbayah is the cultural festival of Banaue, the Ifugao highland town best known for its mountain-carved rice terraces. It is held in the last week of April, when the harvest season is winding down and the weather up in the Cordillera is dry enough for outdoor competitions and street parades. The main program runs around the town proper.

Banaue sits about 350 kilometers north of Manila, deep in the Cordillera mountains of Ifugao Province. It is a small town built around terraces that rise roughly 1,500 meters above sea level. Most travelers come in by overnight bus from Manila, then jeepney into the centro. During Imbayah week, the plaza, the municipal hall grounds, and the main road become the festival venue. Ifugao families from the surrounding barangays come down in full traditional dress, with gongs and chants moving from one street to the next.

The festival mixes ritual and competition. Some parts are sacred, like the boar sacrifice performed by elders. Other parts are loud and playful, like the wooden scooter race, the rice pounding contest, and the men's volleyball game played in traditional loincloths.
why_go:
Imbayah is one of the few large festivals in the Philippines where the entire program is built around a single indigenous group's living culture rather than a Catholic patron saint. If you have been to Sinulog or Ati-Atihan and you want to see something on the other end of the spectrum, this is it. The dances, the gongs, the chants, and the costumes all come straight from Ifugao practice, not from a choreographed contingent.

The wooden scooter race is the event most travelers plan around. Riders go down roughly 7 kilometers of mountain road on handmade taltallak scooters, carved from mananor and butbuta wood by the riders themselves or their families. It is fast, loud, and very local. Spectators line the road and the finish area gets packed, so come early if you want a clear view.

The other reason to go is the timing. Late April is dry season in the Cordillera, the terraces are still green from the standing rice, and the bus and jeepney routes from Manila and Baguio are running on normal schedules. You can pair the festival with a Batad day hike or a side trip to Sagada without rearranging the rest of your itinerary.
tip: Book your Banaue homestay or inn at least two months out. The town is small, rooms near the centro fill up first, and during Imbayah week the late April crowd includes both domestic tourists and Ifugao families coming home from Manila and Baguio. If everything in the centro is full, ask about rooms in Batad or along the road to Bangaan and ride a jeepney in.
what_unique:
What sets Imbayah apart is that it is not built on a Catholic feast day. There is no patron saint, no fluvial procession, no novena leading up to it. The festival grew out of the kadangyan rite, the old Ifugao ceremony where a family announced its rise to noble status by hosting a long feast and pouring so much rice wine that the jar overflowed. That image of abundance is still the symbol of the festival.

The costumes and tools are also specific to Ifugao, not generic tribal wear. You will see the wanno and tapis weaves, brass earrings, and rattan backpacks called pasiking. The dances are accompanied by gangsa gongs played in the seated Ifugao style, which is different from the standing Kalinga style some travelers may already know.

The wooden scooter race is the part that has become the festival's signature image. The taltallak has no engine and no metal parts in the running gear. Riders carve them from local hardwood and tune the wheels for grip on the mountain road. It is a piece of Ifugao practical engineering that exists in Banaue and the nearby towns and almost nowhere else.

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Bangus Festival runs the back half of April. Biggest crowd-pulling events land on April 26, 28, and 30.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Held across Dagupan City in Pangasinan, north Luzon. Major events stretch along De Venecia Highway and the Plaza.

facts.label: Famous for
facts.detail: Dagupan set the Guinness record on May 3, 2003 with a 1,007.56 meter grill cooking 10,000 bangus. The Bangusan street party recreates it yearly.

facts.label: Eat
facts.detail: Bangusine cooking contest puts boneless bangus, sisig na bangus, daing, and relleno on every block. Pair with pigar-pigar at Galvan Street.

history.heading: From a quiet balikbayan harvest to a Guinness-record street party
history.body:
Bangus Festival started small. In the early 1990s, then-mayor Al Fernandez put together a simple bangus harvest called Gilon, a welcome activity for balikbayans coming home for the summer. Gilon is the local Pangasinense word for harvesting milkfish from the ponds, and that quiet community ritual is the seed of everything you see today.

The festival was formally launched as the Dagupan City Bangus Festival in 2002 by then-mayor Benjamin Lim, timed with the city charter anniversary on April 30. A year later, on May 3, 2003, Dagupan grabbed the Guinness record for the world's longest barbecue at 1,007.56 meters, beating Peru's previous mark. That single afternoon cemented bangus as the city's identity, and the Bangusan street party has been the festival's signature event ever since.

The religious anchor is the city fiesta of San Juan Bautista, Dagupan's patron, whose feast falls in late June. The April celebration grew out of civic and economic pride in the milkfish industry rather than a saint's day, which is why it leans more food-and-culture than processional.
history.citation_label: Dagupan City Government, Manila Bulletin, Philippine Star

key_events.name: Gilon-Gilon ed Baley
key_events.blurb: Street dance competition joined by all 31 barangays of Dagupan. Choreography mimics how fishermen scoop bangus from the ponds. Held around April 26.

key_events.name: Bangus Rodeo
key_events.blurb: Contests for fastest bangus sorter, heaviest bangus, and best deboner. Loud, messy, very local. Usually April 28.

key_events.name: Bangusine Cook-Off
key_events.blurb: Restaurants and home cooks present original bangus dishes, from sisig na bangus to bangus sushi. Free tasting if you come early.

key_events.name: Kalutan ed Dalan / Bangusan Street Party
key_events.blurb: The big one on April 30. A grill that stretches more than a kilometer along De Venecia Highway, with thousands of bangus cooking at once.

key_events.name: Fun Run for Disaster Awareness
key_events.blurb: Opens the festival weeks. Loops through downtown Dagupan and doubles as a fundraiser for the city's calamity preparedness program.

key_events.name: Charter Day Parade
key_events.blurb: April 30 civic parade marking the founding of Dagupan as a chartered city, with floats from schools, barangays, and the bangus industry.

place_intro:
Bangus Festival is Dagupan City's love letter to the milkfish. The city sits on the Lingayen Gulf in Pangasinan, about four to five hours by bus from Manila, and it has built its identity around bangus farming in the brackish ponds that ring the Pantal River. Every April, the city blocks off its main roads, lights up the plaza, and turns the back half of the month into a long food and culture run.

The festival officially stretches from around April 9 to May 1, but the events most travelers come for are packed into April 17 to 30. You get a fun run, a Bangus Rodeo with sorting and deboning contests, the Gilon-Gilon ed Baley street dance, the Bangusine cooking competition, and on April 30, the Kalutan ed Dalan grill-up along De Venecia Highway. April 30 is also Dagupan's charter anniversary as a city, which is why the closing date never moves.

Dagupan itself is bigger than first-time visitors expect. It is the commercial center of western Pangasinan, with CSI malls, the Cathedral of St. John the Evangelist, Tondaligan Blue Beach, and a working river port.
why_go:
Go for the Bangusan Street Party on April 30. The visual is hard to describe until you see it: more than a thousand one-meter charcoal grills lined up along De Venecia Highway, cooking around 20,000 bangus at the same time. The smoke, the smell of grilled fish over coals, the sound of tongs clanging. This is what put Dagupan in the Guinness Book in 2003 and the city has been recreating it every year since. You can buy a slot on a grill or just walk the line and eat as you go.

The Gilon-Gilon ed Baley street dance a few days earlier is the cultural heart. All 31 barangays send a contingent, and the choreography is built around the motion of scooping bangus from ponds, weighing baskets, and hauling them to market. Costumes lean blue, silver, and aqua to mimic milkfish scales, and the audience picks favorites loudly.

For food travelers, the Bangusine cooking contest is the actual reason to come. You will see bangus done in ways you have never thought of: bangus sisig, daing na bangus with green mango, smoked bangus belly, bangus relleno stuffed with longganisa, even bangus sushi.
tip: Skip cooking your own grill on April 30. Walk the De Venecia Highway stretch around 5 pm, before the crowd thickens, and buy grilled bangus straight from a barangay slot. Bring water and wet wipes, the smoke gets thick. Park at the city plaza side and walk in, tricycles cannot pass the main road that night.
what_unique:
What sets Bangus Festival apart from the bigger Philippine fiestas is that it is not built around a saint or a religious procession. There is no Sinulog-style image, no Ati-Atihan body paint, no fluvial parade for a patron. It is a civic and food festival born from the city's identity as the country's bangus capital, and that gives it a different rhythm. The center of gravity is a kilometer of grills, not an altar.

The Gilon-Gilon ed Baley dance is also distinct because it is choreographed around fishery labor instead of a battle or a courtship. The movements come from actual bangus harvesting, the scooping motion, the sorting, the weighing, even the haggling at market. Costume designers in each barangay study scale patterns and pond colors, so the parade looks more like a moving aquarium than a typical fiesta line-up.

The other unique piece is the timing with the city charter on April 30. Most Philippine fiestas lock onto a saint's feast day, but Dagupan deliberately fused its civic anniversary with the festival climax. That is why the Bangusan Street Party feels less like a religious closing and more like a citywide birthday cookout.

facts.label: When
facts.detail: Main festival day coincides with Labor Day. Activities run through April with the provincial government lining up events almost daily until May 1.

facts.label: Where
facts.detail: Centered on Lingayen Beach and the provincial capitol grounds along Lingayen Gulf. The Banca Parada fluvial parade runs along the Agno River.

facts.label: Meaning
facts.detail: Pista is from Spanish fiesta. Dayat means sea in Pangasinense. The festival is a community thanksgiving for the bounty of Lingayen Gulf.

facts.label: Highlight
facts.detail: Hundreds of decorated fishing boats line the Agno River in a grand fluvial parade, with crews dressing up their boats to depict local legends and folklore.

history.heading: Roots in postwar fishing villages of Lingayen Gulf
history.body: Pista'y Dayat traces its formal beginnings to 1970, though elders in Pangasinan say the practice had already been alive through the 1960s. In the small fishing towns along Lingayen Gulf, especially Lingayen and Alaminos, fisherfolk would gather every first of May to give thanks for the sea's bounty. They would wade into the seawater on that one day, holding onto the belief that the sea could heal ailments of all kinds if you dipped in it on the first of May. As Lingayen and Alaminos started trading more actively with each other, the small village celebrations grew bigger and eventually got picked up by the provincial government. Today the festival keeps that fishing-community core: local fisherfolk still open each Pista'y Dayat with bamboo raft offerings, a quiet ritual that honors the sea's role in sustaining the more than 62,000 fishing families across Pangasinan.

key_events.name: Banca Parada Fluvial Parade
key_events.blurb: Hundreds of fishing boats decorated by their crews float down the Agno River in a grand parade, with some boats acting out local legends like Limahong's 1574 invasion.

key_events.name: Beach Mass and Bamboo Raft Offering
key_events.blurb: Local fisherfolk open the festival with a mass on Lingayen Beach and a bamboo raft offering to the sea, the most solemn part of the week.

key_events.name: Limgas na Pangasinan
key_events.blurb: The province's signature beauty pageant where candidates from different towns compete, one of the most attended nights of the festival.

key_events.name: Sand Sculpting and Bangus Art Exhibit
key_events.blurb: Teams build sand sculptures on Lingayen Beach while a separate exhibit features artwork themed around bangus, the province's prized milkfish.

key_events.name: Trade, Tourism, Food, and Garden Fair
key_events.blurb: Booths set up at the capitol grounds showcase Pangasinan products, bangus dishes, sweets, and crafts from the province's coastal towns.

key_events.name: Lingayen Gulf Coastal Clean-up
key_events.blurb: Usually held on the last day, volunteers and government workers clean the shoreline along the capitol, closing the festival on a stewardship note.

place_intro:
Pista'y Dayat, the Sea Festival, is Pangasinan's biggest thanksgiving celebration to Lingayen Gulf, the body of water that has fed the province for generations. The main day falls on May 1, which lines up with Labor Day, but the provincial government in Lingayen lines up activities almost daily from early April until then. The festival is centered on the capital town of Lingayen, with most of the action on Lingayen Beach and at the provincial capitol grounds across the road.

Lingayen itself is the capital of Pangasinan and sits right on the shore of Lingayen Gulf. History buffs know it as the site where General Douglas MacArthur and the Allied forces landed on January 9, 1945 to begin the liberation of Luzon. The town was founded in 1614, and its name comes from the Pangasinan word lingawen, meaning to look back.

On the ground, the week feels like a fishing-village fiesta that grew into a provincial event. Expect a fluvial parade on the Agno River, a beach mass, food fairs, beauty pageants, sand sculpting, and a coastal clean-up before everyone goes home.
why_go:
Go for the Banca Parada. This is the moment locals come out for: hundreds of fishing boats, each decorated by its own crew, gliding down the Agno River. Some boats take it seriously and act out historical episodes like Limahong's 1574 invasion of Pangasinan. You can watch from the riverbanks for free, and the closer you are to the take-off point, the better the photos.

Go for the food. Pangasinan is bangus country, and during Pista'y Dayat the Trade, Tourism, Food, and Garden Fair at the capitol turns into a parade of milkfish in every form, plus tupig, puto Calasiao, and bagoong from the coastal towns. The fair runs for most of the festival, so even if you only catch one day you will still eat well. The beach mass and bamboo raft offering on the opening morning are also worth waking up early for if you want to see the quieter, more solemn side of the festival.

Go because it is easy to combine with a beach trip. Lingayen Beach itself is right there, and Hundred Islands in Alaminos is about an hour and a half away by van. You can plan the trip so you catch the May 1 main day, then move on to the islands the day after, which is what most Manila-based families do.
what_unique:
What sets Pista'y Dayat apart from most Philippine fiestas is that it is not a saint's day. It is a thanksgiving to the sea itself. There is no patron whose image is paraded through town. The center of devotion is Lingayen Gulf, and the symbolic act is the bamboo raft offering that local fisherfolk float out at the opening. The dipping tradition is also distinct: elders believed that on the first of May, the seawater of Lingayen Gulf could heal ailments of all sorts, only on that one day of the year. You will still see families wading in for that reason.

The festival is also unusual in scale because it is run by the provincial government, not a single barangay or parish. That means the program is bigger than a typical town fiesta, with Limgas na Pangasinan, an orchestra showdown, dancesports, and a solo vocal contest all at the capitol. At the same time, the village rituals are still intact, so you get two layers in one week.

The third distinct piece is the Banca Parada. Most Philippine fluvial processions carry a religious image down a river. Here, the river is the stage and the fishermen are the ones being honored, with boats freely decorated in local themes.
tip: Stay in Dagupan instead of Lingayen if you book late. Dagupan has more hotels and is only about 20 to 30 minutes away by tricycle or jeep, and you will still make the early-morning beach mass on Lingayen Beach. Book your ride back the same night because public transport thins out fast after the May 1 program closes.
